critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The new model outperforms the baseline by six basis points in PR-AUC.
Why wrong: The comparison is between PR-AUC values computed against fundamentally different ground truths. The baseline PR-AUC of 0.214 is computed against 30-day provisional labels. The challenger PR-AUC of 0.220 is computed against 90-day matured labels. These are different label sets — 90-day labels contain additional confirmed fraud cases that were unresolved at 30 days. The delta of 0.006 conflates label completeness with model quality.
Evidence: Recompute baseline PR-AUC using 90-day matured labels on Q1 data. If baseline PR-AUC rises by 0.004 or more, the apparent gain largely reflects label maturation rather than model improvement.

Issue 2
Claim: Q1 and Q2 are interchangeable evaluation windows for fraud ranking.
Why wrong: A documented population shift (merchant onboarding campaign changing transaction size and geography mix) occurred between Q1 and Q2. The challenger was evaluated on a population shaped by this campaign. If the challenger's features are correlated with the new merchant profiles, its apparent PR-AUC advantage in Q2 may not reflect any fundamental model superiority.
Evidence: Evaluate both models on Q1 data with matched 90-day labels. Compare PR-AUC in this controlled setting.

Issue 3
Claim: The fraud operations team's experience is sufficient to judge the six-basis-point gain as meaningful without confidence intervals.
Why wrong: Operational intuition about PR-AUC gains is calibrated on past comparisons that presumably had similar evaluation standards. The current comparison violates the assumption that past benchmarks used consistent evaluation methodology. The intuition about meaningfulness does not transfer to a comparison using mismatched labels and different evaluation periods.
Evidence: Compute bootstrap 95% CI for PR-AUC difference in a corrected matched evaluation to provide objective uncertainty quantification.
defender_raw: The model has more sophisticated features (merchant and device velocity) and the performance improvement is in the right direction. Using mature labels is the more reliable approach. The team's operational context should be respected — they have experience interpreting PR-AUC differences in terms of investigation workload impact. A corrected matched evaluation is the appropriate next step.
adjudication_raw: The defender's position is that the result is directionally encouraging and a corrected evaluation is the right next step. This is essentially a concession that the current evidence is insufficient. On all three issues: (1) label maturation mismatch is conceded as requiring correction; (2) population shift is not addressed; (3) the operational experience argument does not substitute for statistical quantification when the comparison methodology is flawed. Critique wins. The migration decision requires a corrected evaluation.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: PR-AUC computed against different label maturation standards are different quantities
Issue 2: Q1-to-Q2 population shift from merchant onboarding confounds the quarter comparison
Issue 3: operational intuition does not substitute for CI when evaluation methodology is inconsistent

empirical_test.condition: Recompute baseline PR-AUC with 90-day labels on Q1 data; score Q1 with challenger model; compute paired bootstrap 95% CI for PR-AUC difference
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Matched comparison shows gap below 0.003 or CI crosses zero; or baseline Q1 PR-AUC rises by 0.004 when 90-day labels applied
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Matched comparison shows gap at least 0.006 with 95% CI excluding zero
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Matched gap is positive below 0.006 and CI barely overlaps zero
